volumeTitle: Amphitryon. The Comedy of Asses. The Pot of Gold. The Two Bacchises. The Captives
label: The Two Bacchises (Section 4.2.600c - Section 4.4.685)

sections.label: Section 4.2.600c
sections.translation: Who are you ? Tell me .

sections.label: Section 4.2.601b
sections.translation: I ’ m the skill of his body [*]

sections.label: Section 4.2.602
sections.original: Nequam esse oportet cui tu integumentum .
sections.translation: He needs must be a scampish fellow , who has got so worthless a skin .

sections.label: Section 4.2.603
sections.translation: He ’ ll be coming here , swollen up with wrath .

sections.label: Section 4.2.603b
sections.translation: I wish he may burst .

sections.label: Section 4.2.604
sections.translation: Is there anything you want ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.604b
sections.translation: Be off ; you must do so this instant .

sections.label: Section 4.2.605
sections.translation: Good-bye , teeth-cracker .

sections.label: Section 4.2.605b
sections.translation: And you , skin of his body , good-bye ! This matter has come to such a point , that what advice to give my friend about his mistress , I know not ; who , in his pet , has given up all the gold to his father , and not a coin is there that may be paid to the Captain .

sections.label: Section 4.2.610
sections.original: sed huc concedam , nam concrepuerunt fores .
sections.translation: But I ’ ll step aside , for the door makes a noise . O , here ’ s Mnesilochus coming out , and pensive , too .

sections.label: Section 4.3.612
sections.original: Pétulans , protervo íracundo ánimo , indomito incógitato ,
sections.translation: Insolent , of a froward , passionate , ungovernable , inconsiderate disposition , without reason and moderation , am I ; without fine principles of rectitude and honor , hard of belief , powerless to control my passions ,

sections.label: Section 4.3.615
sections.original: malevolente ingenio natus . postremo id mi est quod volo
sections.translation: born of a temper ill-disposed , unamiable and ungracious is my life [*] . In fine , I am that which I could wish others to be . There ’ s not a person more worthless , or more unworthy for the Gods to bless , or for men to love or to associate with .

sections.label: Section 4.3.618
sections.original: inimícos quam amícos aequómst med habére ,
sections.translation: ’ Tis more just that I should have enemies than friends —’ tis more reasonable that the bad should give me their assistance than the good .

sections.label: Section 4.3.620
sections.translation: There ’ s not a person more deserving of all the reproaches that are befitting worthless men , than I , who have given up to my father the money that was in my power . Could this be believed by any one whatever ? Am I not a madman , who have miserably undone myself and the device of Chrysalus ?

sections.label: Section 4.3.625
sections.original: Consólandus hic mist , íbo ad eum .
sections.translation: He must now be consoled by me ; I ’ ll accost him . ( Aloud .) Hallo , Mnesilochus ! what ’ s the matter ?

sections.label: Section 4.3.626b
sections.translation: I ’ m ruined .

sections.label: Section 4.3.626a
sections.translation: May the Gods grant better things !

sections.label: Section 4.3.626ab
sections.translation: I ’ m ruined .

sections.label: Section 4.3.627
sections.translation: Won ’ t you hold your tongue , simpleton ?

sections.label: Section 4.3.627b
sections.translation: Hold my tongue ?

sections.label: Section 4.3.627a
sections.translation: You are not now quite right in your senses .

sections.label: Section 4.3.627ab
sections.translation: I ’ m undone . Many sharp and poignant woes are now arising in my breast . Did I not put faith in that accusation ? Without any cause was I angry with you .

sections.label: Section 4.3.630
sections.translation: Come , come , do have a good heart .

sections.label: Section 4.3.630b
sections.translation: Whence have it ? A dead man is of more value than I am .

sections.label: Section 4.3.631b
sections.original: Militis parasitus venerat modo aurum petere hinc ,
sections.translation: The Captain ’ s Parasite came here , just now , to fetch the gold hence . I drove the fellow away from this door and from this fair one , and packed him off through my harsh language .

sections.label: Section 4.3.633b
sections.original: Quid mi id prodest ? quom ipse veniet ,
sections.translation: But of what service is that to me ? Wretch that I am , what shall I do ? I have nothing ; and he certainly will carry her off .

sections.label: Section 4.3.635
sections.translation: By my faith , if I had the means , I ’ d engage not .

sections.label: Section 4.3.635b
sections.translation: I know you ’ d give it me ; I know your ways ; and were you not in love , I should now have the very greatest possible confidence in your assistance . At present , you yourself have more than enough of your own business , for me to suppose that you , who are in want yourself ; are able to give me any assistance .

sections.label: Section 4.3.638a
sections.original: Tace modo : deus respiciet nos aliquis .
sections.translation: Do but hold your tongue : some Divinity will favour us .

sections.label: Section 4.3.638ab
sections.translation: Nonsense .

sections.label: Section 4.3.639
sections.translation: Do stay .

sections.label: Section 4.3.639b
sections.translation: What ’ s the matter ?

sections.label: Section 4.3.639c
sections.original: Tuam cópiam eccam Chrysalum video . tace .
sections.translation: Why , I see Chrysalus here , your main resource .

sections.label: Section 4.4.640
sections.original: Hunc hóminem decet auro éxpendi , huic décet statuam statui éx auro ;
sections.translation: It ’ s right this man ( pointing to himself ) should be worth his weight [*] in gold : it ’ s fair that a statue of gold should be erected for him . For , this day , two exploits have I achieved ; with twofold spoils have I been graced . So cleverly have I gulled my elder master this day , that he has been made a fool of . The crafty old blade , by my crafty tricks , have I compelled and constrained to believe me in everything .

sections.label: Section 4.4.645
sections.translation: Then , for the son of the old gentleman , my master here in love , together with whom I drink , with whom I eat and go a-courting , I have procured regal and golden trea sures , that he may take from thence at home , and not go seeking abroad . Those Parmenos [*] and those Syruses ,

sections.label: Section 4.4.650
sections.original: quí duas aut trís minas aúferunt eris .
sections.translation: please me not , who filch some two or three minae from their masters . There ’ s nothing more worthless than a servant without skill — worthless , if he has not a breast mightily well-stocked , so that , whenever there is necessity , he may draw his supply from his own breast . No one can be a person well to do ⋯

sections.label: Section 4.4.655
sections.original: nisi qui ét bene et mále facere tenet .
sections.translation: unless he understands both how to do good and how to do evil . With rogues he must be a rogue ; with thieves let him filch whatever he can . It befits him who is truly wise , to be a person that can shift his very skin [*] .

sections.label: Section 4.4.659_660
sections.original: péctus quoi sapít : bonus sít bonis , malus sít malis ;
sections.translation: Good with the good let him be , bad with the bad : just as things are , so let him ever frame his humour . But I should like to know how much gold my master has taken for himself , and what he has given up to his father .

sections.label: Section 4.4.665
sections.original: sí frugi est , Hérculem fécit ex patre :
sections.translation: If he is a prudent person , he has made a Hercules [*] of his parent : he has given him the tenth part , and has kept back nine for himself . But , see ! the person whom I was looking for ; he meets me most opportunely . ( To MNESILOCHUS .) Has any of your money fallen down , my master , that thus , in silence , you are looking down upon the ground ?

sections.label: Section 4.4.669
sections.original: quid vós maestos tam trístesque esse cónspicor ?
sections.translation: Why do I see you two sad and sorrowful ?

sections.label: Section 4.4.670
sections.original: non plácet nec temere est étiam . quin mihi réspondetis ?
sections.translation: I like it not ; and ’ tis not without some reason . Are you going now to give me any answer ?

sections.label: Section 4.4.671
sections.translation: I ’ m undone , Chrysalus .

sections.label: Section 4.4.671b
sections.translation: Perhaps you took too little of the gold .

sections.label: Section 4.4.672
sections.original: Quam , malum , parum ? immo vero nimio minus multo parum .
sections.translation: How , a plague , too little ? Why , yes , indeed , a very great deal less than too little .

sections.label: Section 4.4.673
sections.original: Quid igitur , stulte ? an tu , quoniam occasio ad eam rem fuit
sections.translation: Why the mischief then , simpleton , since by my skill an opportunity was procured for that very purpose , that you might take as much as you pleased ,

sections.label: Section 4.4.675
sections.original: sic hoc digitulis duobus sumebas primoribus ?
sections.translation: did you thus take it up with the tips of two fingers [*] ? Or , didn ’ t you know how rarely an opportunity of that kind presents itself to a person ?

sections.label: Section 4.4.677
sections.translation: You are mistaken .

sections.label: Section 4.4.677b
sections.original: At quidem túte errasti , cum parum immersti ampliter .
sections.translation: Why , surely , ’ tis you yourself that were mistaken , when you dip ’ t your fingers in not deep enough .

sections.label: Section 4.4.678
sections.original: Pol tu quam nunc med accuses magis , si magis rem noveris .
sections.translation: I ’ faith , you ’ d upbraid me more than now you do , if you knew the matter better . I ’ m undone .

sections.label: Section 4.4.679b
sections.original: Animus iam istoc dicto plus praesagitur mali .
sections.translation: My mind is now foreboding further mischief from those words .

sections.label: Section 4.4.680
sections.translation: I ’ m ruined .

sections.label: Section 4.4.680b
sections.translation: How so ?

sections.label: Section 4.4.680c
sections.original: Quia patri omne cum ramento reddidi .
sections.translation: Because I ’ ve given all up to my father , with every particle [*] of it .

sections.label: Section 4.4.681
sections.translation: Given it up ?

sections.label: Section 4.4.681b
sections.translation: Given it up .

sections.label: Section 4.4.681c
sections.translation: What , all ?

sections.label: Section 4.4.681d
sections.translation: Every jot .

sections.label: Section 4.4.681e
sections.translation: We are done for . How came it into your mind to do this deed so foul ?

sections.label: Section 4.4.683
sections.original: Bacchidem atque hunc suspicabar propter crimen , Chrysale ,
sections.translation: I had a suspicion , Chrysalus , by reason of a charge , that Bacchis and he ( pointing to PISTOCLERUS ) had been playing me false ; for that reason , in my anger , I gave up all the gold

sections.label: Section 4.4.685
sections.translation: to my father .
